case_type_note: gtmstack running on itself. The tool diagnoses its own GTM. This is the founder's case — the inputs reflect the founder's framing of demand evidence, which by Mom Test logic means the inputs are presumed contaminated. The Big Three rubrics apply unchanged. The eval value is in whether independent frameworks (Crossing the Chasm, Obviously Awesome, Predictable Revenue, Play Bigger, The Mom Test) converge on what gtmstack should do next, and whether the convergent diagnosis matches what a thoughtful operator would do anyway. Anyone running gtmstack on a live GTM problem they actually face is the source of truth for what the tool is good at and bad at. No paying customers exist as of audit. No external operator has independently validated synthesis quality. Treat this as an audit, not a victory lap.

company.name: gtmstack
company.description: Open-source Claude Code skill pack that runs canonical GTM books (Crossing the Chasm, Obviously Awesome, Predictable Revenue, Play Bigger, The Mom Test) as runnable evals against real GTM situations. Multi-book synthesis is the value layer. Built by one part-time author. v0.2 as of Q2 2026. Public repo, free, no API key required, runs through Claude Code's headless mode.

current_state.revenue_arr_usd: $0. No monetization. Open-source under MIT license. Free for any Claude Code user.
current_state.customers_count_approx: Zero paying customers. One non-paying author/heavy user (the project author). Unknown external installs — no telemetry wired up. Public GitHub repo made public Q1 2026; star count, clone count, and fork count exist but have not been treated as evidence. Issues opened by external users: zero as of audit. Pull requests from external contributors: zero as of audit.
current_state.customer_profile: Hypothetical target customer profile (no validated ICP yet): GTM leads at Series B/C SaaS or AI dev tools companies who are 'in the chasm' and trying to choose a beachhead; founder-CEOs running their own GTM at <50 employees; growth/strategy consultants augmenting their work with structured frameworks; portfolio-GTM operators at VC firms running clinics for portfolio companies. None of these profiles has been validated by a paying customer or by an operator running gtmstack on a live problem they faced.
current_state.sales_motion: Zero sales motion. README pitches the tool as '~25 minutes from first prompt to deliverable, vs. 3 weeks of strategy consulting at $20K-$50K.' Distribution: GitHub repo public, README walkthrough, occasional LinkedIn posts by the author. No outbound. No demos. No pricing page. No commercial offering.
current_state.geography_primary: Global. English-only. Public GitHub. No localized version. No regional outreach.

expansion_thesis: Get the first 10 external GTM operators running gtmstack on live GTM problems they actually face (not the showcase cases the author authored). Validate that the multi-book synthesis prescribes moves they would take, not just moves they would nod at. Establish 'books-as-evals' as a category — the gtmstack repo as the canonical example, the pattern (framework.md / prompt.md / rubric.json / cases/) as the reusable shape. Decide by end of 2026 whether to commercialize (hosted version, eval marketplace, custom-book-authoring service, GTM-clinic-as-a-service) or maintain as a high-quality open-source side project. The author wants to make this a real thing if there's real demand; the author will not force it if there isn't.
signals: The author has run gtmstack on two showcase cases (cursor-2026, anthropic-brazil-2026). Author's judgment: both syntheses 'feel right' and 'align with each company's observable strategic moves.' The author also wrote the framework encodings, the prompts, the rubrics, the case files, AND judges the outputs. No external operator has independently validated this. The author is grading their own homework.

Public GitHub repo made public Q1 2026 after a private polish pass (security scan, language cleanup for insider-implying phrasing, README walkthrough write-up). No README badges showing real adoption metrics — star count and clone count have not been treated as either evidence or fluff in any deliverable to date.

LinkedIn posts about gtmstack draw modest engagement (low hundreds of impressions, single-digit reactions, occasional 'this is cool / I'd love to try this' replies). No replies have converted to running gtmstack on a live GTM problem. No replies have converted to paid usage.

The /gtm-office-hours skill is installable as a Claude Code skill. Install instructions are in the README. Install count: not measured. Active-user count: not measured. End-to-end completion rate (skill-invoked → synthesis-document-produced): not measured. The instrumentation gap is itself a signal.

Two anecdotal mentions: a friend at a Series B AI startup said 'I'd run this on our GTM next week.' Another friend at a YC-network startup said 'this is the kind of thing we'd pay for.' No follow-through has occurred — no live-problem run, no introduction to a peer, no pricing inquiry, no contract.

Demand-signal hypothesis (load-bearing): GTM leads at Series B/C startups who are stuck on 'we're in the chasm and don't know which beachhead to commit to' will value a multi-book diagnostic in 25 minutes vs $20K-$50K and 3 weeks of strategy consulting. This hypothesis has not been validated by a single prospect committing time, money, or political capital to running gtmstack on a live problem.

Competitive-positioning hypothesis (load-bearing): gtmstack occupies a slot no competitor occupies — the only tool that runs canonical GTM frameworks as runnable rubrics with multi-book synthesis. Tactical AI tools (Lavender, Outreach, Default.com) automate execution but do not diagnose strategy. Generic AI assistants (ChatGPT, Claude.ai) can answer GTM questions but do not encode prescriptive frameworks as rubrics. Strategy consulting (BCG, Bain, McKinsey) offers depth but not speed. AI-strategic-thinking peers (uncertain — possibly nothing exists yet) would be the closest comp. This positioning has not been tested with prospects.

Project artifacts as of audit: 5 books encoded (Crossing the Chasm, Obviously Awesome, Predictable Revenue, Play Bigger, The Mom Test), ~30 case files across books, 11+ persisted run reports, 3 multi-book synthesis deliverables (cursor-2026, anthropic-brazil-2026, plus this one in progress), v0→v1 strict-mode rubric upgrade complete (9.5pp discrimination band on Sonnet vs Haiku on calibration case), cross-judge mode shipped, public-repo polish complete. These are build outputs, not demand outputs.

Known weaknesses (the author's own audit): no live-customer feedback, no independent verification of synthesis quality on real GTM problems, no paid usage, no commitment-ladder advancement past 'polite interest' from any prospect, no measured install or active-use rate, no analyst engagement, no press, no organic search traffic.

Pricing model: gtmstack is open-source + free. Runs use headless Claude Code, which requires a Claude Code subscription (~$20/mo Pro or $200/mo Max) — that subscription is the user's, not gtmstack's. No gtmstack-specific pricing exists. No pricing has been tested with prospects. Monetization candidates the author has considered (untested): hosted version with team features, eval marketplace, custom-book-authoring service, GTM-clinic-as-a-service, certification.

Author availability: ~10-15 hrs/week part-time. Not a full-time founder. No team. Not raised. The author has not committed to making gtmstack a company; it could remain an open-source side project indefinitely. This is a structural constraint on any GTM strategy that requires sustained outbound, customer success, or rapid iteration in response to feedback.

Brand awareness: zero outside the author's LinkedIn audience and a small overlap with the Claude Code community. No press coverage. No analyst engagement. No organic search traffic above noise. Author is well-known in a small startup-founder corner of the internet but not known as a GTM thinker or strategist.

The /gtm-office-hours skill, when invoked, walks through a structured 10-minute interview before running the books. Skill quality is high (modeled on gstack's office-hours skill, which the author has used and validated). No end-to-end external user has reported the experience. The skill could be perfect; it could be broken; the founder cannot tell from inside.

Inspiration / shape match: gtmstack is patterned on gstack (Garry Tan's Claude command pack for builders). gstack has visible adoption in startup-founder circles. gtmstack inherits the shape but not the audience. The hypothesis that gstack's audience overlaps with gtmstack's audience has not been validated.

Specific past behavior the author HAS observed in self: the author runs gtmstack on questions that come up in advisory conversations and finds the synthesis useful for structuring the author's own thinking. This is an n=1 use case where the founder is the user. It is not customer evidence; it is dogfooding.
notes_for_eval: This case is the most adversarial-to-inputs case in the gtmstack repo because the author authored everything — the books, the rubrics, the case files, the deliverables, and now this self-diagnostic case. The Mom Test should be the most active book on this case; the others should engage their normal frames but be alert to founder-framing-as-evidence. The eval value of this case is in the convergent-vs-divergent pattern: do the 5 books agree on what gtmstack should do next? If 4-5 books converge on the same prescription, that prescription is the highest-confidence move for the author. If the books diverge sharply, the divergence itself is the diagnostic — gtmstack has no clear next move and the author needs more inputs.
